data.title: 10 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of Nearly 9% (September 2025)
data.description:
Note: We have recently changed the format of this monthly article from 5 Best CEFs to 10 Best CEFs. This will provide more flexibility to readers to choose the funds that suit their individual needs and risk tolerance. Also, the focus of the selections will not be on the current valuation so much, but on other attributes that reflect the best value for the long term.

===

Introduction:

The S&P 500 (SP500) has been making new all-time highs, almost on a regular basis. However, so much of the gains have been concentrated in the large technology and high-growth names. So, obviously, the closed-end funds, or CEFs, that have exposure to equities of high-growth and technology names have done better than others. But at the same time, some other sectors have also performed well, such as Utility funds, MLPs, and some foreign country-specific funds such as Taiwan Fund (TWN), Mexico Fund (MXF), and MS India Investment Fund (IIF). Also, the Fed just announced a 25-basis point rate cut, and more will likely follow. This should help the CEFs in general, but specifically, funds that carry significant leverage.

All that being said, it is hard to find anything that has both the quality and the low valuation, so buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P 500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.20%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Also, the method of raising income by selling shares is generally too stressful and full of pitfalls, and that’s why it is not recommended. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher dividends than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the "5 Best DGI Stocks." You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs to provide our readers with a select list of 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance ( at least on a relative basis), and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield of at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of just under 9% (as of 09/18/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 15.7% and 15.4% (annualized) in the last three and five years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is decent, at nearly -5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of ten probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization > 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/history longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we were generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article are sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down to 50 Funds

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top five.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium:

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say, a 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (with the most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 09/15/2025)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate:

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-term Return on NAV (Last Three Years):

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. Please note that the huge drawdowns in the case of MLPs during the pandemic (caused by the Oil crash) and subsequent recovery have led to too many MLPs and energy funds topping this list (3-year NAV gains). For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-year Annualized Return on NAV:

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions vs. Earnings):

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds, as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list on coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution:

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of the three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point:

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 27 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: AIO, BXSY, CSQ, EDF, EIC, EMO, EOD, EOS, GGN, PEO, USA, and VVR (12 duplicates).

Appeared three times: KYN, XFLT (4 duplicates).

Appeared four times: PDX (3 duplicates).

Appeared five times: STK, TWN (8 duplicates).

Once we remove 27 duplicate rows, we are left with 48 (75 - 27) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, a high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

Criteria

Max. Weight

Comment

Baseline expense

5.0

Current distribution rate

7.5

Excess discount/premium

5.0

3-YR NAV return

5.0

5-YR NAV return

5.0

10-YR NAV return or 10-YR total return

5.0

If there are less than ten years of history, an average of three- and five-year returns is taken.

Excess NAV return over distribution rate

5.0

Adjusted Coverage Ratio

7.5

This factor is adjusted for certain factors. The adjustment is performed based on the type of fund, such as equity and sector funds.

Once we have calculated the weights, we combine them to calculate the "Total Combined Weight," also called the "Quality Score."

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 48 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_10_Best_CEFs_-_Sep_2025.xlsx

10-positions Portfolio of the Month

Before we get to the final five, we will narrow down our list to 20 names. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 5 lists, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the list of the top 20 selections (from different asset classes) in the order of sector:

Table 8:

10-best CEFs of the Month:

So far, we have followed our filtering criteria to a large extent. However, in the end, if we only want to select ten funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment to some degree. While we narrow down this list to ten names, we would be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of sectors and asset classes. Since this step is somewhat subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Here's the list of the top 10 selections this month (in order of sector):

(CCD), (STK), (PDX), (EOD), (KYN), (BANX), (GGN), (UTF), (XFLT), and (USA).

Table-9:

Some information about the selections:

We will only briefly describe the selected funds. Since there are 10 of them, we will not be providing a very detailed analysis of them.

Here are some details on our 10 selections:

Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income - CCD:

The Fund primarily invests in a portfolio of convertible securities, debt securities, and other income-producing securities, including equities. Investments include common and preferred stocks, investment-grade and below-investment-grade bonds, loans, equity-linked notes, and floating-rate securities. It also uses high leverage, exceeding 30%. It pays a monthly distribution that currently yields roughly 11%. The premium is almost insignificant currently and compares very favorably with its very high premiums over the last 12 months and 3 years. Moreover, the lower interest rates in the future should help this fund. However, on the negative side, there is some chance that the management would reduce the distribution payout as it has a negative NII (net investment income) as well as negative income from operations (NII + realized and unrealized capital-gains). So, investors need to be somewhat cautious and only invest on a dollar-cost basis.

Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund - STK:

The fund invests in a portfolio of high-growth technology stocks with an option overlay strategy designed to mitigate downside volatility and generate income. The fund writes call options on the NASDAQ 100 Index or its exchange-traded fund equivalent on a month-to-month basis. The aggregate notional amount of the call options could typically range between 40% and 50%. STK's yield is at best moderate at 5.35% for a closed-end fund, but the total returns are highly impressive, even beating many pure-play growth funds.

PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund - PDX:

This month, in the top 10, we include one of PIMCO's newest funds. This fund appeared near the top in four out of our seven categories. The reason it stands out is that it has performed remarkably well in the last three and five years. The fund is only about six years old, so it does not have a long history. Some of its past performance can be attributed to its timing of inception (in 2019) and initial allocation to the energy sector. In fact, this fund was known as the "PIMCO Energy & Tactical Credit Opportunities Fund (ticker - NRGX)" until Nov. 2023. Since the beginning of 2024, PIMCO has decided to diversify the fund from an energy-focused fund to a mix of energy and multi-sector Credit/Bonds. Unlike other PIMCO funds, its distribution yield is quite low at close to 7%, but PIMCO has aggressively raised the dividends in the last two years. The fund is offering a decent discount of -7% compared to premiums on other similar PIMCO funds.

Allspring Global Dividend Opportunity Fund - EOD:

The fund's primary investment ​goal is to seek a high level of current income, with a secondary goal of long-term growth of capital. The fund is invested roughly 80% in global equities with a focus on dividends, and the rest in fixed income (18%) and cash equivalents (2%). The equity portion is invested in 70-80 companies spread across sectors and regions. The fund also employs an options strategy to enhance income and to generate gains on call options. The fund also uses a modest leverage of roughly 15%.

The allocation to the U.S. is roughly two-thirds of the total, and the rest is invested in countries such as the U.K., France, Japan, China, Germany, Italy, Canada, Taiwan, and Brazil. The largest sector is information Technology at 28%, followed by Financials at 18% and Communications at 11%.

Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund - KYN:

It is one of the popular MLP funds invested in the midstream companies. The MLP funds have had an incredible run in recent times. They are showing very high returns on a 3-year and 5-year basis due to the energy price crash during the 2020 Covid period. However, they do not score very well on a 10-year return basis. That being said, they are also scoring well in our rankings because of attractive yields and discounts. Currently, KYN is yielding close to 8% and offers nearly a 10% discount. One could also look at another MLP, NB Energy Infra and Income Fund (NML).

ArrowMark Financial Corp - BANX:

The fund invests in preferred equity, subordinated debt, convertible securities, regulatory capital relief securities, and, to a lesser extent, common equity. The primary objective is to provide a steady, high income and, more or less, a stable NAV.

The fund offers a well-covered distribution that is reasonably high at 8.1% and is fully supported by the NII (net investment income) while maintaining a stable (or growing) NAV. It offers exposure to floating-rate instruments that have benefited from the rise in interest rates, which may appear to be a negative right now. But rates are not going to fall fast enough, and it is highly unlikely that we will see zero interest rates any time soon. We should also point out that the fund did reasonably well even during the previous zero-interest rate environment, and the management appears to be confident that it can maintain the NII. However, it may be noted that while the fund raised the distribution early this year, it has been lowering the distribution now, but gradually (which has fallen below last year’s levels). So, the management is quick to adapt to changing conditions and make sure that the distribution is fully covered.

GAMCO Global Gold, Natural Resources & Income Trust - GGN:

This is a fund invested in companies involved in precious metals like gold and silver, as well as energy and natural resources. In the last year or so, gold and other precious metals have done exceedingly well, and that is reflected in the growth story of this fund. It is believed that the bull run in the precious metals is not over yet, and there is still much room for further growth. Depending upon your opinion on where we may be in the precious metal prices, you should or should not invest in this fund. As such, this fund provides a nice way to earn high income while participating (and diversifying) in precious metals. The fund has roughly 30% exposure to energy companies as well. The fund currently yields 7.25% and trades at less than 1% premium.

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund - UTF:

This is one of the most popular Utility and Infrastructure funds out there in the CEF space, besides the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG). However, UTF appears to offer better value currently compared to UTG.

The fund invests in infrastructure companies, which consist of utilities, pipelines, toll roads, airports, railroads, ports, and telecommunications companies. The fund offers close to 7.5% yield and a small discount of roughly -2.50%. The fund offers a very reliable distribution payout, as it has paid consistent and growing distributions since its inception.

XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust - XFLT:

This fund is a bit of a controversial choice and is probably the riskiest out of the top 10. The fund is for investors who truly seek very high income. As it is often said, the higher the income yields, the higher the risk. So, please use due diligence. In a portfolio of 10 funds, as a whole, the risk may be contained. Moreover, this fund may balance out other names that are all heavy on the equity side. The income yield is obviously very attractive at nearly 16%. This fund can be used as a means of diversification and elevating the income yield for the overall CEF-based portfolio.

This fund is somewhat complex to understand, but this is how the fund describes itself:

"The Trust invests in a dynamically managed portfolio of floating-rate credit instruments and other structured credit investments within the private markets. Under normal market conditions, the Trust will invest at least 80% of managed assets in senior secured loans, CLO debt, and CLO equity."

The fund is currently invested to the extent of 46% in Senior First-Lien Loans, which are considered relatively safer. However, it also invests nearly 50% of its assets in CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations) and nearly 3/4 of those in CLO equity tranches, which are considered the riskier part of CLOs. Its closest competitor fund in its space is Eagle Point Credit (ECC).

It is important to note that the fund has reduced the monthly distribution amount twice since December last year. That is also part of the reason that the fund's premium has turned into a sizable discount of roughly -9%.

Liberty All-Star Equity - USA:

The fund seeks total investment return by investing in large-cap equity holdings, with 90% invested in the U.S. market. Its returns are comprised of long-term capital appreciation and current income. The fund is highly diversified and holds roughly 140 positions. It invests at least 80% of its assets in equity securities. According to the fund's literature, "The fund strategy combines three value-style and two growth-style investment managers." This fund has been in business since 1986 and has a pretty good record of “nearly” matching the performance of the S&P 500 over different time periods. However, the main advantage for income investors is the consistent 10% income (2.5% of NAV every quarter). However, the distribution can vary from quarter to quarter as the NAV would fluctuate. Since the fund is actively managed, uses no leverage, and has a 0.90% fee, it does underperform the S&P 500 slightly, from 0.5% to 1.5% over long periods of time.

CEF-Specific Investment Risks

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees.
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market).
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV).
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts

The broader market, as represented by the S&P 500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.20%. Many income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight the best closed-end funds for investment each month. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts. We have also provided a larger list of ten CEFs from many different asset classes.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of just under 9% (as of 09/18/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 15.7% and 15.4% (annualized) in the last three and five years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is decent, at -5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Please note that these selections are based on our proprietary rating system and are dynamic in nature. So, they can change from month to month (or even week to week). At the same time, some funds can repeat from one month to the next if they remain attractive over an extended period. Furthermore, note that if a fund has not made it to our list, it does not mean it is not good. It could be because they may not be attractively priced at this time or trading at a premium while running our filtering process.

.

data.title: Vistry: Labour Ambitious About Affordable Housing
data.description:
Vistry (OTCPK:BVHMF) could actually be quite interesting. The idea here is that it's a homebuilder with a structurally lower overall capital intensity compared to average homebuilders as it will pre-sell to housing associations (registered providers, local authorities and private renting companies) through their partnered sales programme, primarily focused on government sponsored affordable housing initiatives. Government money is injected into registered providers in particular to buy and assure affordable housing often in mixed tenure communities. Presuming the UK government can finance it, there are initiatives to push forward with much more funding for these affordable housing initiatives. Even under conservative governments these initiatives have been amply supported. There is some macro exposure on the open-market-derived minority of sales, not helped by non-materialised cuts as the markets might have hoped for, and there have also been idiosyncratic issues with a geographic division of the company systematically underestimating costs and therefore poorly quoting projects, which will be a headwind to results for the moment but will attenuate in the coming years. But FCFY is already quite high at around 7% and should have scope to improve with the company producing homes below its capacity.

Results Discussion

Just briefly on the business model in the context of some of the latest results.

The company develops homes and sells them. However, since they are providing housing substantially for affordable housing initiatives (mandated Section 106 housing + discretionary additional affordable housing provision as part of their developed mixed-tenure residential communities), they are able to pre-sell homes to "partners", meaning registered providers of housing i.e housing associations, which reduces their net working capital burden significantly and upfronts cash. This comes at the cost of margin but it's great for their cash profile, particularly when there is risk of projects getting delayed, which has been a threat as housing associations occasionally go it alone with land-led grant-based developments and otherwise curtail spending (starting with less profitable and more burdensome S106 housing) amid strains on economy and finance. The PRS segment is more private oriented but it still works through their partners whereas the open market works with the more regular economic profile of a homebuilder. 89% of the partner-based revenue is forward sold for FY 2025.

While there could theoretically be issues with housing associations deciding to draw back investments in affordable housing projects and focus on more economical initiatives, the current government is preparing a significant increase in investing in housing supply initiatives, an important voter issue, following the spending review and the newly named Social and Affordable Housing Programme with an earmarked 39bn GBP. Vistry therefore foresees greater affordable housing in the business mix, less PRS, and less open market but with new projects quoted at more appropriate margins, therefore not sacrificing margin incrementally despite the negative mix effect. PRS this H1 was down mainly due to comp effects, though it is expected to remain a smaller part of the mix. Moreover, the company has indicated that it is producing housing at volumes that are maybe around 15-20% below its capacity frontier. Affordable housing has been a key political issue in the UK for a while, and even under conservative governments there has been a legitimate focus on growing housing supply as well since the issue of insufficient supply is so acute.

Past year's finances had to be restated for under-forecast costs related to developments occurring in the Southern Division affecting cost of sales. Therefore, there is lower margin backlog that will have to get passed through the business:

Roughly, roughly, I would expect about 20% of those sites that were low margin in the first half of the year to complete in the second half of this year. Then something like 30% of them will go during the course of the following year. And the remaining 50% will be skewed more towards the front end of '27 and '28, but will disappear over time. But there will be some tail going out to sort of '29 and '30.

Timothy Lawlor, CFO

The reason for the declines in the H1 is due to the lower volumes in both the open market and partner-based business, but particularly in the partner-related businesses ahead of major decisions in government budgeting causing reticence in new dealmaking amid a somewhat complicated bidding environment for housing associations for S106 properties. There were quite a lot of ongoing S106 developments in the pipeline for HAs to focus on from before, and an environment of constrained financing, less attractive economics and relative attractiveness of land-led grant-based developments (that don't require outside homebuilders) had been putting strain on this important, cash-front market for homebuilders like Vistry. Open market took share in the mix despite a pretty stagnant open market development environment amid persistently high rates. ASPs were up but the decline in volumes and revenues, and therefore negative operating leverage, weighed on results.

Bottom Line

The idea is that new money and effort by government going into affordable housing development is likely allaying reticence in the market in the upcoming H2. There was plenty of backlog and a long waiting list for affordable housing, but just not so much new activity in H1 in the partnered market. Plans for more funds should allow the resumption of housing association bids on S106 housing, which provides essential working capital for many in the homebuilding industry to develop both open market and partnered projects.

Vistry is one of the leading homebuilders that works together with government, even in formal joint ventures with Homes England. ROICs for the partnered business are structurally higher on lower working capital commitments. The burden of lower margin backlog will decline from this year to the next hopefully lifting liquidation margins. FCFY is around 7% based on last year's annual data and forecasts for this year's data, noting that working capital declines tend to reverse in H2 due to the typical timing of completions and dealmaking - cannot just annualise H1 operating cash flows. 7% is a strong base yield to begin with as the company approaches its productivity frontier of around 20k completions per year, up from the 17k annual run-rate managed in the last fiscal year as an example. Moreover, digesting of poorer margin projects and an H2 upswing given political evolutions should also support a decent performance. Vistry is worth following on an incremental improvement in the environment, particularly as the company is quite aggressively buying back shares (7% in the last 12 months). It may be a little soon for a rate cut angle for the open-market business, but it's the partnered business that matters.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TLT: Double Your Yield By Selling Options On U.S. Treasuries
data.description:
Since 2022, long dated government bonds have enjoyed risk-free yields in the 4%+ range.

With treasuries finally providing a meaningful yield - as opposed to the meager rates we experienced in the 2010's - bonds have come back into focus for income investors, especially as inflation appears to be heading lower.

NASDAQ:TLT, the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF, has been a go-to option, as it remains the largest long-dated bond fund on the market. With a solid sampling technique, low overhead costs, and a long track record of success, we think the ETF is an excellent long-dated bond proxy for most portfolios.

But what if you could double your treasury income? What if you could multiply TLT's 4.3% yield while taking even less underlying price risk?

In this article, we're proposing an option trade idea that stands to do just that.

By selling near the money put options on TLT, investors can generate more than double the yield they normally would, while creating a win-win scenario for themselves. If bond prices go up, or remain flat, investors stand to capture significant premiums, and if bond prices go down, then option sellers may end up buying long-term treasuries at an incredibly attractive valuation.

Today, we'll explain why we think bond prices (and TLT by extension) have bottomed out, and lay out a high-yield option trade that stands to double TLT's already generous dividend payout.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

TLT's Construction

First off, let’s take a look at TLT, and explain why we think the fund is a great option for those looking to get involved in long dated treasuries.

For us, TLT’s advantages can really be boiled down to two separate things: the fund's strong liquidity, and the long track record of accurate index sampling.

On the liquidity front, ETFs often achieve secondary market liquidity as a function of their underlying assets under management. For example, imagine we were told to buy $1 billion dollars of S&P 500 exposure. Which ETF would you pick? We'd go straight for SPY, which can support a buy of that size without moving the market. Sure, there are other ETFs, but SPY is the largest, and thus it enjoys the largest market for buyers and sellers of its' shares.

TLT has essentially cornered the market for long-term bond ETFs, as a function of the fact that it is by far the largest ETF by AUM for the category:

Plenty of these ETFs don't do exactly what TLT does, either, which makes it far and away the most liquid way to get exposure to long dated bonds - and their yields.

This is key for reducing transaction costs, even for smaller orders

Secondly, TLT employs a rigorous approach towards tracking the bond indices. TLT primarily follows the ICE ® U.S. Treasury 20+ Year Bond Index, which screens for treasury bonds that have $300 million minimum par value outstanding, excluding balances owned by the Federal Reserve. The index also excludes bonds with variable rates and zero coupon STRIPS:

The Underlying Index consists of publicly-issued U.S. Treasury securities that have a remaining maturity greater than or equal to twenty years and have $300 million or more of outstanding face value, excluding amounts held by the Federal Reserve System (the“Fed”). In addition, the securities in the Underlying Index must be fixed-rate and denominated in U.S. dollars. Excluded from the Underlying Index are inflation-linked securities, Treasury bills, cash management bills, any government agency debt issued with or without a government guarantee and zero-coupon issues that have been stripped from coupon-paying bonds. The Underlying Index is market value weighted, and the securities in the Underlying Index are updated on the last calendar day of each month.

iShares, as a fund overlay, uses a 'representative sampling' technique to manage the fund. This means that TLT may not invest in exactly the same treasury bonds that the index picks, but it tries to find similar exposures with respect to yield, outstanding value, maturity, etc.

As a result, TLT has done a good job mirroring a long-term treasury investment strategy:

For those who want exposure to long dated treasury yields, TLT is simply the best ETF option.

TLT's Outlook

With respect to where we think bond prices could go from here, take a look at the following chart:

It may be hard to read initially, but essentially the chart above shows two separate lines. In white, you can see the federal funds rate over the last 20 years, and in yellow, you can see 30 year bond yields. The chart is split into five separate zones.

Initially, we can see that from 2006 to 2015, interest rates went lower to combat the Great Recession and support the ensuing recovery. 30 year yields trend down over this time as well. From then, starting in 2015 and going through the end of the decade, rates began to rise once again, as the Federal Reserve tried to normalize inflation while rebuilding its 'disaster hedge'.

Over this period, 30 year rates moved marginally higher as well.

Then, in the third zone, you can see the year 2020, when rates fell through the floor to combat the economic fallout from Covid. As investors panicked and bought safe haven assets like 30 Treasuries, yields plummeted as well, and then recovered through mid 2024. This rise in yields coincides with the federal funds rate, which, was hiked to combat post-pandemic inflationary pressures.

Well, the chart may look somewhat messy, it highlights the fact that 30 year yields and central bank rates tend to move in the same direction over time, despite the fact that they don’t always move in the same direction in the short term.

Now, entering a new period where we’ve seen two rate cuts over the course of a year, we're anticipating that bond yields will once again move back down.

This is supported by the fact that inflation, which is currently running at 2.7% YoY, remains well below the federal funds rate, implying that employment concerns (that we talked about in a recent article) remain paramount, at least for the time being:

Taken together, we’re expecting 30 year yields to go lower, following rates over the next year+.

Given that bond prices and rates are inversely correlated, this implies that treasury prices, and TLT by extension, could be ready to move higher:

While an investor might choose to simply purchase bonds for the yield, and hold for the potential appreciation to maturity, we think a more interesting opportunity exists.

The Trade

So - you can purchase bonds right now for a solid level of yield, and moderate appreciation prospects. But what if you could double your yield from exposure to TLT, while simultaneously reducing your price risk?

Enter the idea of a short put option trade.

When you sell put options on a stock or fund, you’re essentially crystallizing your potential upside into a current cash payout that you can withdraw to your bank account. In return for this payout, you’re obligated to purchase shares of a given fund (in this case TLT) if they drop below a certain price over the lifetime of the option.

In this way, you can structure a trade where if bond prices go up or trade sideways, you double your annualized income stream. Conversely, if bond prices slide, then you could be obligated to purchase them at a better price than where they’re currently trading today. This means even more potential upside in the future, an even lower cost basis, and a higher overall dividend.

It’s hard to argue this doesn't appear to be a win-win, especially given where the markets are at.

Specifically, we like the idea of selling December 31st, $88 strike puts on TLT:

These options trade at a price of $2.07, meaning that when you sell one contract, you get to keep $207 as a cash premium. For 10 contracts, you get to keep $2,070, and so on. In order to sell one contract, you need $8,593 of collateral in your trading account, so if the option is assigned, you're able to buy shares of TLT at the assignment price.

Right now, we think the $88 strike options appear optimal because they combine a strong annualized yield of roughly 9.1% (2.4% cash yield over 3.8 periods), with the relatively unlikely event that TLT finishes beneath $88 per share over the next 96 days.

Finally, our confidence in the trade is buttressed by the fact that over the last 23 years, the $83-$85 range has proven to be an incredibly strong technical support level:

With a resulting cost basis of this trade of less than $86 per share if assigned, we think the odds are skewed in option sellers' favor. Thus, our trade idea and our Buy rating.

How We're Wrong

While the option trade idea we’ve selected is a mechanism to juice total yield for investors, the real risk here comes from the fact that a long position (or short put position) is essentially making a 'long' bet on bond prices, which implies an inherent 'short' bet on long dated rates.

Obviously, we’ve laid out our rationale for being long, but in the event that inflation picks back up and rates need to move north, than $85 per share in TLT may no longer prove to be the value it once was, and we’d expect shares to drift down into the 70's range.

Depending on how long rates stay at this level, this could represent a long-term capital impairment in the event the shares are assigned.

Summary

Overall, though, we think selling options on TLT is a great way for investors to juice their exposure to attractive bond yields while entering into a relatively low risk trade.

Of course, rates could move higher unexpectedly over the next few quarters due to a number factors, but we’d argue that this appears unlikely. Overall, we rate TLT a Buy, and think that sharp income investors should take a look at shorting put options with DTE's in the 100 - 150 range.

Cheers!

data.title: Buying A Dollar For 50 Cents: 2 Stocks I Believe Every Long-Term Investor Should Consider
data.description:
Introduction

I cannot believe how fast time is flying.

Next month, we’ll get to discuss Q3 '25 earnings, as the third quarter of the 2025 calendar year is about to end in a few days. And as long as companies are still required by law to report earnings four times per year, something the president wants to change, we’re closing in on yet another period filled with numbers and comments from every industry imaginable.

On top of valuable intel, we get numbers that are critical to keep the market’s rally alive. After all, as I have written in countless articles, the market trades at nearly 23.0x forward earnings, a number that makes it absolutely crucial for earnings growth to remain high.

For now, the market is quite at ease with this valuation, as analysts expect double-digit EPS growth through at least 2027, as a quick look at the data in the chart below reveals.

Now, to add some more data, FactSet dove into fourth-quarter expectations, as it noted that of 12,351 ratings on S&P 500 stocks, 55.8% were buy ratings. Just 5.3% of all ratings were sell ratings. For what it’s worth, the five-year averages for these numbers are 55.2% and 5.4%, showing that the current situation is far from unique. It’s boring, one could say.

The thing that isn’t boring is how analysts assess various industries.

Using the same source, we find that analysts prefer energy, as this industry has 65% buy ratings. To be honest, I am quite surprised, as energy is, by far, my favorite industry due to its poor performance (attractive valuation) and favorable long-term fundamentals (see this article for details).

Needless to say, analysts haven’t gone "full contrarian," as their second and third-favorite industries are information technology (64% buy ratings) and communications services (63%). Essentially, both of these industries are "tech." Moreover, consumer staples and utilities are the most disliked industries, as the overview below shows quite clearly.

I also have to say it’s fascinating to see the variety among companies with the highest percentage of bullish ratings. This includes Walmart (WMT), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Broadcom (AVGO), and Nvidia (NVDA). I cannot say this surprises me.

Interestingly enough, this also includes beaten-down companies like Danaher Corp. (DHR), the medical equipment giant, Diamondback Energy (FANG), one of the most successful oil producers in the Permian Basin, and VICI Properties (VICI), one of my favorite REITs at the moment.

The list of companies with the biggest sell rating percentage is also quite diverse, as it includes asset manager Franklin Resources (BEN), which has been lagging behind many of its larger peers for a while; utility giant Consolidated Edison (ED), industrial dividend grower Illinois Tool Works (ITW), and building products leader Lennox International (LII).

Furthermore, the interesting thing is that the market is currently trading roughly 3% above the average year-end target. Since 1999, only two years have had similar returns where analysts lagged the market by this much in September (1999 and 2024), as Bloomberg reports. The same report also came with the chart below, which shows how wrong analysts were in Q4 '24.

As most of you may remember, that’s when President Trump won the election. While it’s fair to say not everyone is a fan of the president, the market was convinced it would cause an extended market rally, fully supported by deregulations.

The opposite happened, as tariffs caused the market to crater.

Then, the market realized things weren’t that bad as expected, as tariff fears eased, the Fed started cutting rates, economic growth remained steady, and the AI rally fueled Big Tech.

Unfortunately, the rally that followed has caused the risk/reward to turn highly unfavorable, as I have written in countless articles, including these two (here and here). The quote below sums it up perfectly:

“The bull market in equities is ‘alive and kicking’,” said Craig Johnson at Piper Sandler. “While we maintain our bullish outlook over the intermediate- to longer-term, we also recognize that the SPX has advanced for nearly five straight months without a material pullback.” - Bloomberg.

That’s why I also promised to provide more actionable ideas in these articles, which is what the second part of this article is all about, as I provide two companies that I expect to have a realistic shot at doubling in the years ahead, as cheesy as that may sound.

So, to justify the title of this article, I’ll explain why these two are such standouts, which is based on much more than just a low multiple.

Let’s dive in!

Carlisle Companies Incorporated (CSL) - One Of My All-Time Favorite Dividend Growers Trades At A Huge Discount

Carlisle is a company I have brought up a few times this year. That’s no surprise, as it has done quite poorly. Year-to-date, the company has lost nearly a quarter of its value, which is a lot for a company that has a terrific long-term track record.

As we can see below, despite its poor performance, the company has still returned 295% over the past ten years, trailing the market by roughly 10 points. After losing a quarter of its value, while the market is trading at an all-time high, supported by Big Tech, I have to say this isn’t that bad.

With that said, I’m not making excuses. Lagging the market isn’t the best news, and as this website is called Seeking Alpha, we want to generate market-beating returns.

Luckily, this downtrend opens up terrific opportunities, as Carlisle isn’t down because of mismanagement or any problems related to its business. No. That’s as strong as ever.

See, Carlisle is a company at the center of one of my favorite theses. That thesis is long-term commercial and residential construction/remodeling. As boring as this may sound, I believe this is one of the most favorable opportunities over the next decade, as just 30% of all non-residential buildings in the U.S. are younger than 25 years. 55% are older than 35 years.

As most buildings were constructed before China’s entry into the World Trade Organization shook up American manufacturing, many are now in desperate need of modernization. This includes climate-proofing.

Carlisle has positioned itself perfectly to serve these markets, as it has used divestitures in recent years to become a pure-play supplier of "innovative building envelope products and solutions for more energy-efficient buildings," to quote its investor presentation.

Using last year’s data, the company generated 74% of its revenue from construction materials. The remaining sales were weatherproofing technology sales. More than 60% of all sales went to replacement and remodeling operations. Commercial operations accounted for 82% of its revenue. Meanwhile, 90 cents of every revenue dollar was generated in the U.S.

Using third-party data, the company estimates it serves a $70 billion market, which would imply it has a market share of just 7%, leaving a lot of room for organic and acquired growth. That’s also why the company is consistently buying smaller companies to enhance its product portfolio.

While I would not call Carlisle a mass-acquirer, it is starting to look that way, as it uses its free cash flow to integrate new companies, improve their operations, and grow its own free cash flow again. It’s a favorable growth "flywheel."

On top of that, the company’s management is terrific, as it has a return on invested capital of nearly 30%, which is proof of its ability to effectively put money to work. It is also high, according to any comparison, as a quick industry comparison shows.

Moreover, even during the Great Financial Crisis, it was able to grow its margins. During the pandemic, it kept margins elevated and boosted its revenues. Last year, it ended up with 29% adjusted EBITDA margins, 17 points more compared to 2008.

So, why is Carlisle down so much?

The answer is really simple: the market is afraid of cyclical construction-related stocks in this market, as interest rate uncertainties and really sluggish cyclical growth don’t bode well for its Vision 2030 plans, which see at least $40 in EPS, based on 5% annual organic revenue growth, >25% adjusted EBITDA margins, and a free cash flow margin of no less than 15%.

Although Carlisle has not made the case that Vision 2030 is in danger, comments made during its Q2 '25 earnings call clearly confirm weakness in its key markets:

The new construction market has softened somewhat for both commercial and residential segments since our April Carlisle market survey. On the residential front, although the survey indicates a mid-single-digit decline in new construction activity, our resilient approach and adaptable strategies prepare us to navigate these changes effectively. Despite recent headlines noting challenges such as record high home prices and elevated mortgage rates; we remain optimistic about our strategic pathways to growth. - CSL 2Q25 Earnings Call.

This is what the company said with regard to its 2030 Vision:

Our Vision 2030 targets remain firmly on track despite near-term challenges. We are committed to more than doubling adjusted EPS to $40-plus by 2030, maintaining industry-leading ROIC of 25% and achieving free cash flow margins exceeding 15%. With the target organic revenue CAGR of over 5% and anticipated cumulative free cash flow exceeding $6 billion, we have multiple pathways to achieve our $40 of adjusted EPS as committed to in Vision 2030. The actions we're taking today, ranging from operational improvements and strategic acquisitions to innovation investments, position Carlisle to bridge current performance to these long-term objectives, all the while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation. - CSL 2Q25 Earnings Call.

Unfortunately, right now, the market doesn’t believe Vision 2030.

And, to be honest, that’s OK.

It’s a cyclical company, which means headwinds almost always result in downgrades.

The good news is that the value proposition remains terrific. On top of having a terrific track record when it comes to its ROIC and margins, it has an investment-grade balance sheet (BBB rating) and a 1.3% dividend that has seen 49 years of consecutive dividend growth.

Since 2011, the company has grown its dividend by 14% per year.

Currently, it trades at less than 17.0x earnings, below its ten-year average of 20.5x. Although I fully agree with anyone who makes the case that 20.5x is too much during downcycles, even in this environment, analysts see 13% EPS growth in 2026 and 8% growth in 2027.

For Vision 2030 to turn into reality, the company will have to grow its EPS by slightly more than 12% per year from its 2024 base of $20.20. That’s possible. However, even if the company were to fail its Vision 2030 by 15% and report only $34 in EPS, it would have a fair value of no less than $680, 102% above the current price based on a 20x multiple.

This may also explain why the company has boosted its buybacks, as its Board authorized another 7.5 million shares in buybacks, roughly $2.5 billion at current prices. That’s roughly 18% of its market cap.

Hence, if we get a stronger rebound in cyclical growth, I believe the market will reward CSL again. It would also do wonders for its EPS outlook.

That’s why I think we’re dealing with a $680 to $800 stock over the next 5-7 years (base case).

Devon Energy Corporation (DVN) - A Super Cheap Energy Opportunity

This oil and gas producer was one of the first upstream energy stocks I started covering many years ago. I always liked it and its peer, Marathon Oil, which is no longer a standalone company.

However, I haven’t covered the company in a long time, as it failed to convince the market it had what it takes to compete with much more efficient drillers in areas like the Permian Basin. This includes Diamondback Energy, which has used aggressive M&A and excellent operations management to make itself one of the biggest success stories in U.S. energy.

Over the past ten years, FANG shares have returned 175%. Devon returned 30%, outperforming the SPDR S&P Oil & Gas Exploration & Production ETF (XOP) by slightly more than ten points.

In general, Devon has failed to generate any shareholder value since the start of the U.S. shale revolution after the Great Financial Crisis. As we can see below, massive gains before 2008 abruptly ended in roughly 17 years of high volatility. This was triggered by the high-growth rates of oil production. Supply boomed so much that demand growth could not keep up. That’s why the shale revolution turned into a blessing for the economy (low inflation) and a headwind for producers.

To make matters worse, Devon Energy did not have the benefits of being a pure-play producer in the Permian like Diamondback. It also focused too much on production growth.

On top of that, slightly more than half of its production consists of non-oil output. Especially in the Permian, prices for natural gas are extremely low due to rising associated gas production (that happens when the quality of oil wells declines) and not enough pipeline capacity.

While all of these were good reasons to ignore the company and buy a peer like Diamondback, my bullish view on energy (find out more in this article) and the company’s improved operations are reasons for me to return to Devon.

For starters, the company’s operating position is favorable, as 59% of its production comes from the Delaware Basin, the sub-basin of the Permian with the most reserves. Especially close to the New Mexico/Texas border, we find some of the best drilling spots in the U.S.

On a side note, that’s also where companies like LandBridge (LB) focus on, which I own. In fact, Devon Energy closed a deal with LandBridge to get access to important pore space in the years ahead. This is needed to dispose of produced water, an increasingly big problem for oil and gas producers (see this article).

Devon is also a major owner of WaterBridge (WBI), the biggest water infrastructure company in the Permian (and a partner company of LandBridge).

In this area, just 27% of its output is natural gas. The remaining output is oil (46%) and high-margin natural gas liquids, which should get even better margins as pipeline capacity improves.

Meanwhile, it has made great progress on key points like capital efficiency improvements, cost reductions, and overall drilling improvements. This includes drilling improvements of up to 10% in areas like the Williston and Delaware. It also has a net debt ratio of a mere 0.9x, $1.8 billion in cash, nearly $5.0 billion in liquidity, and an investment-grade rating of BBB.

On top of that, Devon has a lot of capital return potential.

As we can see below, even at $75 WTI, it can generate $3.7 billion in free cash flow. That’s 17% of its current market cap. In other words, if it were to return all of its free cash flow, it could pay a 17% yield or buy back 17% of its shares. That’s how much this is.

On a full-year basis, it targets a cash return of 70% through buybacks and dividends.

Currently, it pays $0.24 in quarterly dividends per share. That’s a base yield of 2.8%. Last year, it also paid a special dividend in each of the first three quarters.

I expect this special dividend to return in the years ahead, as I believe oil will work its way up to $85 and likely beyond. As we just saw in the free cash flow chart, this would substantially boost its return potential.

With regard to its valuation, a 17% implied free cash flow yield at $75 WTI is enough reason for me to believe this company can double if I’m right about oil.

Since the pandemic, Devon has traded close to 15x free cash flow. This implies a 7% free cash flow margin. Hence, at $75 WTI, it could trade at a $56 billion market cap, 150% above the current price. At higher oil prices, I foresee bigger gains, with more support from buybacks, which improve the per-share value of the business.

Eventually, I expect DVN to recapture its 2022 highs again in the years ahead.

Takeaway

We’re getting very close to Q3 '25 earnings, and I couldn’t be more excited.

Unfortunately, the market environment is challenging, as valuations are high, which means there’s little room for error when it comes to the performance of the market’s most critical stocks.

The good news is that there is still plenty of value to be found if we look a bit closer.

In this article, we discussed two standout stocks. One industrial giant with secular growth in commercial building remodeling and a terrific dividend growth track record, and one undervalued energy stock I expect to benefit from a widespread energy and commodity rebound.

In the 4-5 years ahead, I believe both stocks have highly realistic potential to at least double. Devon could even triple if oil makes its way to $85 and beyond.

I own Carlisle. I do not own Devon due to my concentrated portfolio and investment in Permian landowners.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risks are cyclical risks. Carlisle could continue to suffer if construction remains weak. Meanwhile, Devon requires higher oil prices for the market to regain confidence in U.S. shale producers.

On top of that, Devon has much more volatility than Carlisle. Whereas Carlisle is a consistent dividend grower, Devon is a highly volatile oil and gas driller. Please take that into account when doing your own due diligence.

data.title: Park Aerospace: Waiting For The Juggernaut (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Park Aerospace (NYSE:PKE), a tiny aerospace and defense supplier with significant growth prospects, has been one of the companies that I initially marked as a buy. I initiated coverage in 2022 with a buy rating, and the stock has shown a 91% total return against the S&P 500’s gain. Since my last coverage, in which I had a hold rating for Park Aerospace, the stock has gained 32%, outperforming the S&P 500’s 10.5% gain. I downgraded the stock from buy to hold in June 2024 for the simple reason that Park Aerospace was falling from one issue into the other and had to deal with staff shortages, inflation, customer timing, and destocking, and to me at some point it started feeling that for an aerospace company, Park Aerospace was always too busy shifting blame rather than constructing risk mitigation plans. It left the company as a speculative buy opportunity that in the near future had always faced more headwinds than tailwinds.

In this report, I discuss the Q1 2026 earnings and assess whether the company is worth an upgrade to a buy rating.

Park Aerospace Margins Improve Despite Lower Sequential Sales

First quarter sales of $15.4 million came ahead of what Park Aerospace indicated. We also saw that gross margins were above the 30% that the company generally aims for and that is a big positive given that the company did not reach >30% margins since Q2 FY24. Margins still had a bit of a headwind from C2B fabric sales. The company is the exclusive distributor of Ariane’s RAYCARB C2B fabric. The company can sell the C2B fabric to defense customers; however, these are lower margins compared to turning these fabrics into ablative materials. During the quarter, the company still had $1.1 million in fabric sales against $0.48 million in ablative material sales. So, that provides a bit of a margin headwind. However, since the Q1 sales matched the value of production, the gross margins were quite good overall. When value of production is relatively low to sales, the inventory costs have to be amortized over a lower sales value of production, and the margins go down. That impact that Park Aerospace previously suffered was not there in this quarter. The company did have $0.275 million in mixed shipments, slightly up compared to the prior quarter. The positive for Park Aerospace was that it booked $6.2 million in GE Aerospace jet engine program sales. Annualized that is $24.8 million, which is just slightly higher than FY25. However, the company estimates Q2 sales between $6.7 million and $7.2 million. The company expects full-year sales for the GE Aerospace programs to be between $28 million and $32 million. EBITDA of $2.96 million came in at the higher end of the guided range with margins of 19.2%

Park Aerospace Navigates A Set Of Opportunities And Challenges

For Park Aerospace, I believe that the prospects have improved. They already were good, but we are also seeing more tangible results on what Park Aerospace has been seeing for years as their primary growth drivers.

The first opportunity is what Park Aerospace has coined as the Juggernaut. They are the GE Aerospace engine programs contracted by MRAS under a long-term agreement. The prospective run rate of those programs is north of $61 million per year. So, revenues from the GE Aerospace engine programs can more or less double from the levels anticipated for this year. The growth is primarily driven by the upside potential for the Airbus A320neo program. Airbus has been seeing challenges hiking production, which has delayed the significant uptick in sales for Park Aerospace. However, we note that we are seeing some positive indications in the delivery stream in recent months. The other growth driver is the Boeing 777X program with a $11.25 million opportunity as part of the Juggernaut.

Separately, the company now has a $5 million run rate long-term agreement with GE Aerospace. This contract is separate from the MRAS agreement.

The other growth opportunity is on the RAYCARB sales. In recent quarters, there was a headwind as a customer had to go through a requalification. Requalifications are standard procedures when there is technical change to a production or change of supplier. Completion of that requalification was nearing by the time Park Aerospace held its quarterly call, and I expect that by now the requalification has been completed, which should help Park Aerospace sales. Furthermore, the demand for missile and missile defense systems should provide medium-term growth opportunities for Park Aerospace’s ablative materials. The company has received a blanket purchase agreement for $40 million for RAYCARB. A BPA is a prearranged simplified contract for recurring products and services orders. Just shipping the fabric will only provide low-margin incremental profits to the company. However, if Park Aerospace is also selected to turn the fabric into ablative materials, that provides a substantial growth opportunity.

So, what we are seeing is that next to the Juggernaut there are other sales for RAYCARB and potentially high margin ablative materials rolling in as well as the new $5 million annual run rate agreement with GE Aerospace. It makes the company more diversified from a revenue point of view and gives the company more growth channels.

What Park Aerospace Expects For Q2 2026

For the second quarter, the company is expecting $15 million to $16 million in sales, which at the midpoint points at more or less stable revenues and down from the $16.7 million in sales in the same quarter last year. So, I am not really impressed by that. EBITDA is expected to be between $3 million and $3.4 million. The mid-point of the range is in line with the same quarter last year. So, at lower sales the company expects higher margins, and that is a more positive way to look at the guidance.

A More Bullish Demand Mix For Park Aerospace

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The investment case for Park Aerospace is not the most solid one and that is primarily because the stock is already more or less fairly valued with what I am expecting for next year. Additionally, higher stock prices are dependent on margin expansion in combination with ramping up revenues from the A320neo program and the GE Aerospace program. Ramping up program revenues is what actually has been a major challenge for Park Aerospace. Not because the company does not have the capacity, but because airplane production rates have been falling short of targeted rates for quite some time. So, that introduces some uncertainty in the stock price modelling where I assumed that the MRAS LTA will generate higher revenues in the years ahead and the GE Aerospace program will ramp up as well. The company, however, has an additional lever in the form of the RAYCARB C2B fabric. The blanket purchase agreement of $40 million is an example of that. I have not modelled that in yet, but we see that in missile and missile defense there is significantly higher demand these days and that combined with space applications may increase sales for RAYCARB C2B and more importantly the higher margin ablative materials when processed by Park Aerospace.

So, next to the “Juggernaut”, we see additional growth levers that could provide support for higher stock prices. As a result, I am marking the stock a speculative buy. It was already a speculative buy previously, but I tend to mark speculative buys a hold rating as they are not necessarily convincing buys yet. For now, I do believe while the buy remains of speculative nature there are more growth programs to actually be a little more convinced about the prospects of the company.

Conclusion: Park Aerospace Remains A Speculative Buy

For Park Aerospace, I maintain a speculative buy rating but more into buy rating territory now as there are more growth programs that may increase sales in the years ahead. If the company performs well on contracts, I believe the company could also attract more business. The challenge for Park Aerospace remains the timing of program ramp ups.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VVR: Deeply Unloved And A High Discount To NAV
data.description:
Thesis

The last time we covered the Invesco Senior Income Trust (NYSE:VVR) was in the beginning of the year, after a two standard deviation move in the price that saw the CEF plummet by more than -12% in one day. At the time we described the violent move in light of the fund's history, and outlined a short dated trade to take advantage of the overextended move. The CEF proceeded to bounce afterwards, validating our view.

A lot has happened since in the leveraged loan market, especially given the recent Fed cut, so we are going to revisit this name and its analytics in the overall macro context.

Leveraged loan CEFs are now deeply unloved

The closed end fund market expresses its 'love' for a certain segment via premiums to NAV. When that sector falls out of favor, we get this:

As we can see from the above chart that graphs VVR, the Nuveen Floating Rate Income Fund (JFR), and the Eaton Vance Floating Rate Income Trust (EFT), the market is now assigning deep discounts to all leveraged loan CEFs. The highest fall from grace is attributed to VVR, which at some point in the beginning of the year had a massive +6% premium to net asset value.

But let us see what VVR has done historically for this metric:

In fact, prior to rates moving higher in 2022, we see the CEF trading usually with a discount between -12% and -5%, so the current discount to NAV is smack in the middle of the range. Expect the current dynamic to persist going forward since the market is pricing over 100 bps in cuts going into 2026. Lower Fed Funds means lower yields for leverage loans, which in turn should reduce the yield on VVR.

Macro outlook for Fed Funds

The Fed cut rates on September 17, 2025, and currently the SOFR futures market is pricing in 100 bps more in cuts by the end of 2026:

The above graph plots the futures market for 1-month SOFR and the Fed Dot Plot. While the 'Dot Plot' is subject to debate, change, and political nominations, the SOFR futures market is not. The market is showing us deep cuts to come in the next year, with 1-month SOFR at 3% by December 2026.

Leveraged loan market - spreads are tight but price gap-downs high

Let us first start with spreads, which are historically tight:

As per LCD, the average credit spread paid by a single-B rated borrower (excluding B-minus ratings) has slid to 285 bps over the base rate. That's the lowest level since the Global Financial Crisis in 2008. Continued demand from CLO investors and a lack of M&A transactions are cited as reasons behind this technical set-up.

While spreads are tight, please note that names which encounter troubles 'gap-down' very fast, and they do not bottom at the historic 60% recovery for leveraged loans:

The First Brands term loan basically moved from par to 42 c/$, which is highly, highly unusual. Common equity and bonds get wiped out first, with senior secured term loans usually having recoveries in the 60s and 70s. Not here. One reason behind this move might be the following, as per Bloomberg:

Auto-part supplier First Brands Group LLC is preparing to enter bankruptcy with no restructuring plan in place, a sign of how quickly the company’s fortunes are unraveling, according to people familiar with the situation. It’s unusual for companies as big as First Brands to file for Chapter 11 without a restructuring support agreement with lenders in hand.

The fundamentals, therefore, point towards a market with very little upside but significant and violent downside.

Distribution needs to come down

If we look at the September Section 19a, we see the following:

This CEF does not break it out per fiscal year, so let us look at a couple of months of data:

September: 0.031 interest income, 0.007 Roc

August: 0.03 interest income, 0.0074 Roc

July: 0.0276 interest income, 0.0104 Roc

The above translates into the following table:

So as of now (without lower SOFR) the CEF is using roughly 20% in ROC. This figure will get larger if the distribution is not cut after the recent Fed Funds reduction. Kindly keep in mind there is a lag for 1 month SOFR and 3 month SOFR to embed lower Fed Funds.

The CEF's current distribution of 13.3% is not sustained, and if we completely eliminate ROC from the September figure and annualize the data we get a 10.9% distribution before lower Fed Funds are factored in. We are of the opinion that a sustained distribution into the new year is somewhere around 10% after the lower Fed Funds percolate into the holdings. This is still a very high and attractive figure to have.

The CEF is a leveraged take on bank loans

The fund continues to represent a leveraged take on bank loans:

The name is overweight single-B credits, and has a large not-rated balance. The CEF continues to invest mainly in 1st lien loans, but as we saw with First Brands, 1st lien loans can gap down as well:

As an investor in a bank loan CEF you want to ensure the vehicle is focused on 1st lien paper. VVR comes with a high 35% leverage ratio, which will amplify moves if credit goes south.

Conclusion

The recent widening in the discount for VVR is warranted given lower Fed Funds, the market outlook for SOFR, and the fundamental picture. Market participants are now pricing the CEF as an instrument with limited upside but a high downside. However, when looking back historically, the fund is in the middle of its usual range during lower rates, thus it is correctly valued in our view from a discount perspective. We are of the opinion that VVR is now within a fair value range, but would be hesitant to add to geared bank loans amid very tight fundamentals.

data.title: One Of The Best 11% Yields To Buy Today: Fidus Investment
data.description:
Fidus Investment (NASDAQ:FDUS) is a phenomenal business development company, as evidenced by its stellar long-term total return performance.

Additionally, when you combine its 11% dividend yield with its long-term track record of growing book value per share and paying a stable and even growing dividend per share over time, FDUS appears to be not only one of the best BDCs, but indeed one of the very best 11% yields anywhere in the market that you can buy today, especially when you combine that with its solid balance sheet and sustainable base dividend profile.

In this article, I will detail why.

Fortress Balance Sheet

FDUS has one of the best balance sheets in the BDC space with a regulatory leverage profile of just 0.5 times and a total leverage ratio of just 0.8 times, both of which are among the lowest in the BDC space. Additionally, its net asset value per share has remained quite stable and has actually increased over time. Even over the past year alone, it has increased from $19.50 to $19.57 today. Year-to-date it has improved from $19.33 to $19.57 despite paying out a hefty dividend.

The company's debt is also well-laddered, with some maturities termed all the way out to 2035. All of its debt is at fixed rates and carries a blended cost of just 4.7%. Additionally, it has significant liquidity between its $140 million revolving credit facility and $91 million in cash on hand, enabling it to invest opportunistically and minimizing the risk of any sort of financial distress.

High-Quality, Defensive Portfolio

Its portfolio is high-performing and well-diversified with positions in 92 different companies, of which 82.5% of its portfolio is in secured debt at a very attractive weighted average yield of 13.1%. Only 0.5% of the portfolio's fair value is on non-accrual, which is one of the lowest non-accrual rates in the sector. Its average borrower interest coverage ratio of 3.1 times is also quite conservative. Furthermore, 12% of the portfolio is invested in equity, which gives it upside potential via gains in its equity investments that can help sustain and grow its net asset value per share over time.

Its high performance over time is largely due to the very selective underwriting that management performs, as it ends up investing only about 2.8% of roughly 600 opportunities it evaluates each year.

Shareholder-Friendly Fee Structure

The company's base management fee is not particularly attractive, but its high 8% income incentive fee hurdle rate is among the very best in the industry, putting it in the same league as Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) and ahead of peers like Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), which uses a 7% fee hurdle rate, and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which uses a meager 6% hurdle rate. This means that more income is left over for shareholders to enjoy via dividend payouts.

This has helped contribute to its very attractive average annual return on equity of 12.5%, which is among the very best in the BDC space.

Sustainable, Generous Dividend

The company's dividend setup is quite strong. In Q2, it paid out a $0.43 per share base dividend alongside a $0.11 per share supplemental dividend, which gave it a $0.54 per share total payout against a $0.57 per share adjusted net investment income. Looking at its base dividend and annualizing it gives it an 8.5% yield that is covered by adjusted net investment income per share with a very conservative 1.33 times coverage ratio, giving it one of the most conservative base dividend coverage ratios in the BDC space.

Meanwhile, taking into account its supplemental dividend and annualizing it gives it an 11% dividend yield, which is not only fully covered by adjusted net investment income to the tune of a 1.06 times coverage ratio but also gives it among the most attractive payout profiles in the entire BDC space.

Additionally, its $1.39 per share in undistributed spillover income is equal to about three-quarters of dividends, which puts it on very conservative footing, especially given that its leverage ratio is low enough that it could increase leverage quite easily if needed. This also gives it considerable leeway in the event that the Fed begins to cut interest rates at a more aggressive clip, thereby positioning it to keep paying its dividend even under challenging circumstances for the sector.

Valuation And Market Positioning

In terms of valuation, FDUS does not jump out as being particularly cheap, as it trades at a small 3% premium to NAV at the moment. Compared to some other blue chip stocks in the sector, this does not make it particularly cheap. For example, ARCC trades at a 3% premium to NAV, while BXSL trades right in line with net asset value per share. Additionally, compared to its own history of typically trading right in line with NAV, FDUS does not look particularly cheap at the moment either.

Investor Takeaway
